eco_baseline_footprint.formula: BIF = (vCPU x 15 EUR) + (Storage GB x 0.08 EUR)
eco_baseline_footprint.method_note: RAM is considered included in compute cost and is not monetized separately in the operationalized framework model. The adopted MCC assumes a non-pruned validating full node with full blockchain replication for structural comparability with other replicated-ledger technologies.
eco_baseline_footprint.source: Bitcoin Core full node requirements; framework normalized cost model

ps_throughput.source: Bitcoin throughput literature and Bitcoin mainnet baseline transaction capacity estimates

eco_variable_transaction_cost.conversion_note: Indicative benchmark-period average transaction fee derived from Bitcoin fee statistics and converted to EUR using the benchmark exchange-rate convention.
eco_variable_transaction_cost.source: YCharts Bitcoin Average Transaction Fee; ECB USD/EUR exchange rate

eco_storage_footprint.interpretation: Baseline persistent storage allocation associated with the adopted MCC host for a validating Bitcoin full node, not the minimum disk occupancy of a pruned or otherwise specialized node configuration.
eco_storage_footprint.source: Bitcoin Core full node requirements; framework comparative baseline assumption

parallel_execution.motivation: Bitcoin does not provide native parallel execution of independent ledger operations at Layer 1.

horizontal_partitioning_sharding.motivation: Bitcoin does not include native sharding or horizontal partitioning of ledger state.

absence_of_mandatory_global_serialisatio.motivation: Global serialization through a single canonical blockchain remains structurally required.

scalable_coordination_model.motivation: Coordination remains globally serialized through Proof-of-Work and longest-chain consensus.

ps_scalability.source: Bitcoin Developer Guide; throughput and blockchain architecture literature

append_only_structural_history.motivation: Bitcoin maintains an append-only blockchain history of committed blocks and transactions.

cryptographic_linkage_between_records.motivation: Each block header includes the hash of the previous block and commits to transactions through a Merkle root.

cryptographic_write_authentication.motivation: Transactions are authenticated through digital signatures and script-based spending conditions.

independent_integrity_verification.motivation: Validating full nodes can independently verify ledger validity and integrity.

rci_tamper_evidence_auditability.source: Bitcoin Developer Guide; Bitcoin block reference; Bitcoin transactions reference; Bitcoin full node documentation

protocol_enforced_finality.motivation: Bitcoin does not provide protocol-enforced irreversible finality in the sense captured by this methodology; committed history may in principle be reorganized under sufficient adversarial hash power.

multi_party_confirmation_requirement.motivation: Transaction confirmation depends on continued chain extension through distributed mining participation rather than on unilateral acceptance by a single actor.

absence_of_unilateral_rollback_capabilit.motivation: No ordinary single actor can unilaterally revert confirmed Bitcoin history under the native protocol model.

rci_finality_rollback_resistance.assumption_note: FRSF captures structural rollback resistance under standard Bitcoin mainnet protocol assumptions and should not be interpreted as a complete adversarial security proof against all possible mining, network, or censorship attacks.
rci_finality_rollback_resistance.source: Bitcoin Developer Guide; Nakamoto consensus model

absence_of_mandatory_single_writer_depen.motivation: Bitcoin does not rely on a single structurally privileged writer node for transaction inclusion or chain progression.

quorum_based_write_continuation.motivation: Write continuation is sustained as long as a sufficient subset of mining participation remains active to extend the chain under the native protocol model.

automatic_write_continuation_mechanism.motivation: The protocol continues operation automatically without centralized failover procedures as long as mining participation persists and network connectivity is maintained.

single_node_failure_survivability_of_wri.motivation: Failure of a single node does not structurally disable the system's ability to accept and confirm transactions.

rci_write_availability_fault_tolerance.assumption_note: WASF measures structural write-continuity properties under standard Bitcoin mainnet protocol assumptions and does not claim immunity against all possible liveness, censorship, or network-partition attacks.
rci_write_availability_fault_tolerance.source: Bitcoin Developer Guide; Bitcoin full node documentation

absence_of_unilateral_state_authority.motivation: No single entity can unilaterally determine the valid Bitcoin ledger state under the native protocol model.

structural_requirement_for_multi_entity_.motivation: Valid chain progression and transaction confirmation emerge from distributed mining and validation under common consensus rules rather than from unilateral control by a single actor.

distributed_control_over_validator_admis.motivation: Participation in transaction validation and block production does not require discretionary admission by a central authority.

absence_of_immutable_control_concentrati.motivation: The participant set is open and not structurally locked to a single authority, with control distributed across an evolving set of miners and validating nodes.

ags_control_distribution_governance.source: Bitcoin full node documentation; Bitcoin Developer Guide; Nakamoto consensus model

native_privilege_differentiation_capabil.motivation: Bitcoin does not provide a native privilege-differentiation model for organizational roles or enterprise access governance.

native_identity_lifecycle_governance_cap.motivation: Bitcoin does not provide a native identity lifecycle governance capability for creating, revoking, or modifying platform identities and privileges.

native_access_governance_enforceability.motivation: Identity and access governance are not enforced through a protocol-native access-governance layer.

ags_identity_access_governance.source: Bitcoin transactions reference; Bitcoin Developer Guide

non_unilateral_compromise_requirement.motivation: Compromising Bitcoin consensus outcomes is not a unilateral action for an ordinary single actor under the native protocol model.

majority_compromise_requirement.motivation: Successful history-rewriting or majority attacks require control of a majority of active hash power.

supermajority_compromise_requirement.motivation: Bitcoin does not require a supermajority compromise threshold for the relevant consensus attacks captured by this methodology; the critical threshold is majority hash power rather than a two-thirds-type supermajority.

ags_collusion_resilience.assumption_note: CRSF is assessed as a structural compromise-threshold proxy under Bitcoin mainnet protocol assumptions, not as a complete adversarial analysis of all bribery, censorship, or mining-centralization risks.
ags_collusion_resilience.source: Bitcoin Developer Guide; Nakamoto consensus model
